---
title: "02 - Random Forest code examples"
format: html
editor: visual
number-sections: true
number-depth: 3
toc: true
---



# Outline


- Regression example with Superior Red Lake of the Woods
- Pretend it's 2001 (birth year of Random Forests!), trying to predict the future
- Use out of bag performance to tune and evaluate the model
- Fit example model with defaults, (maybe) look at variable importance in example
- Show trees and leveling off with training error - can't overfit OOB error
- model tuning - loop over m (1:p)
- fit final model
- interpret model - varimp and pdps (maybe interaction strength with H-stat)
- See how well we predicted


Bonus example:
- wing length correlated var's example
- show same prediction accuracy, different var imp

Bonus example 2:
- group sampling in random forest to deal with repeated measures - stratification (stratified sampling)


## Random forests in R
Random forests can be built with a number of packages in R. We're going to use the `randomForest` package in this workshop, but some others can be found in this table (from Claude).

| Package | Best for | Pros | Cons | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `randomForest` | Learning, smallŌĆōmedium datasets | + The classic, well-documented reference implementation<br>+ Simple, stable API<br>+ Built-in `varImpPlot` and `tuneRF`<br>+ Works seamlessly with `caret` / `tidymodels` | ŌłÆ Single-threaded ŌĆö slow on large data<br>ŌłÆ High memory use (stores all trees)<br>ŌłÆ No native support for sparse matrices | Moderate |
| `ranger` | Production use, large datasets | + Multi-threaded (set `num.threads`) ŌĆö much faster<br>+ Low memory footprint<br>+ Survival, regression, and classification<br>+ Drop-in replacement for `randomForest` syntax | ŌłÆ Fewer built-in diagnostics / plot helpers<br>ŌłÆ Variable importance less flexible than `randomForest` | Fast |
| `h2o` | Very large data, distributed computing | + Distributed / cluster-ready (runs on Spark, AWS)<br>+ Auto-handles categorical encoding<br>+ Built-in cross-validation and grid search<br>+ Supports extremely large datasets | ŌłÆ Requires a running H2O JVM cluster<br>ŌłÆ More complex setup; heavier dependency<br>ŌłÆ Data must be converted to H2O frames | Very fast |
| `Rborist` | High-dimensional data | + Very fast on wide (high-p) data<br>+ Memory-efficient tree storage<br>+ Parallel by default | ŌłÆ Less mature ecosystem and documentation<br>ŌłÆ Fewer output diagnostics<br>ŌłÆ Smaller community / fewer Stack Overflow answers | Fast |
| `partykit` (cforest) | Unbiased variable selection | + Conditional inference trees ŌĆö unbiased variable importance<br>+ Better handling of mixed-type / correlated predictors<br>+ Rigorous statistical framework | ŌłÆ Noticeably slower than ranger / randomForest<br>ŌłÆ Less intuitive API for beginners<br>ŌłÆ Fewer ecosystem integrations | Slow |





# Back to our red fish example

**INSERT PIC**

We're going to use our red fish example from Superior Red Lake of the Woods again, but this time, we're going to model
